[[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[category{Uji-Kabane system]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:氏姓制度]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Uji-Kabane system}][[Category:qq]] [[ja:氏姓制度]]

{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: - '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. - '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. - '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. - '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).

This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: - '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. - '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. - '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. - '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).

The titles included: - '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. - '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. - '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. - '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).

{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: - '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. - '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. - '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. - '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).

{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: - '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. - '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. - '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. - '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).
== See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * [[Eight Ranks of Titles]] * [[Ritsuryō system]]
== References == {{reflist|20em}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Naoki, Kōjirō |title=Ancient Japanese Clans and the Emperor |publisher=[[Hanawa Shobō]] |date=1964 |isbn=978-4-8273-1012-2 |ref=Naoki 1964}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |title=The Ancient Japanese Clan and Title System |publisher=[[Yagi Shoten]] |date=2009 |isbn=978-4-8406-2036-9 |ref=Nakamura 2009}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |editor=Sato, Makoto |title=Themes in Japanese Ancient History: Politics and Diplomacy |publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]] |date=2020 |isbn=978-4-642-08384-3 |ref=Nakamura 2020}}

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: - '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. - '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. - '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. - '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).
== See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * [[Eight Ranks of Titles]] * [[Ritsuryō system]]
== References == {{reflist|20em}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Naoki, Kōjirō |title=Ancient Japanese Clans and the Emperor |publisher=[[Hanawa Shobō]] |date=1964 |isbn=978-4-8273-1012-2 |ref=Naoki 1964}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |title=The Ancient Japanese Clan and Title System |publisher=[[Yagi Shoten]] |date=2009 |isbn=978-4-8406-2036-9 |ref=Nakamura 2009}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |editor=Sato, Makoto |title=Themes in Japanese Ancient History: Politics and Diplomacy |publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]] |date=2020 |isbn=978-4-642-08384-3 |ref=Nakamura 2020}}

{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: - '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. - '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. - '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. - '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).
== See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * [[Eight Ranks of Titles]] * [[Ritsuryō system]]

{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: - '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. - '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. - '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. - '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).
== See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * [[Eight Ranks of Titles]] * [[Ritsuryō system]]

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: - '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. - '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. - '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. - '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).
== See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * [[Eight Ranks of Titles]] * [[Ritsuryō system]]
== References == {{reflist|20em}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Naoki, Kōjirō |title=Ancient Japanese Clans and the Emperor |publisher=[[Hanawa Shobō]] |date=1964 |isbn=978-4-8273-1012-2 |ref=Naoki 1964}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |title=The Ancient Japanese Clan and Title System |publisher=[[Yagi Shoten]] |date=2009 |isbn=978-4-8406-2036-9 |ref=Nakamura 2009}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |editor=Sato, Makoto |title=Themes in Japanese Ancient History: Politics and Diplomacy |publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]] |date=2020 |isbn=978-4-642-08384-3 |ref=Nakamura 2020}}

{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: - '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. - '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. - '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. - '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).
== See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * [[Eight Ranks of Titles]] * [[Ritsuryō system]]

The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: - '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. - '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. - '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. - '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).

These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: - '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. - '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. - '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. - '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).

{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:qq]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: - '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. - '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. - '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. - '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).

This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).

{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:qq]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: - '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. - '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. - '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. - '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).
== See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * [[Eight Ranks of Titles]] * [[Ritsuryō system]]

{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:qq]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: - '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. - '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. - '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. - '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).
== See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * [[Eight Ranks of Titles]] * [[Ritsuryō system]]

{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:11]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).
