Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:11]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).
== See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * [[Eight Ranks of Titles]] * [[Ritsuryō system]]

{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢|1=Nobi}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:11]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The titles included: * '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.

This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).

This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).
== See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * [[Eight Ranks of Titles]] * [[Ritsuryō system]]

{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢|1=Nobi}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:11]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).
== See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]

The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).== See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]

{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢|1=Nobi}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:11]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字).== See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]
== References == {{reflist|20em}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Naoki, Kōjirō |title=Ancient Japanese Clans and the Emperor |publisher=[[Hanawa Shobō]] |date=1964 |isbn=978-4-8273-1012-2 |ref=Naoki 1964}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |title=The Ancient Japanese Clan and Title System |publisher=[[Yagi Shoten]] |date=2009 |isbn=978-4-8406-2036-9 |ref=Nakamura 2009}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |editor=Sato, Makoto |title=Themes in Japanese Ancient History: Politics and Diplomacy |publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]] |date=2020 |isbn=978-4-642-08384-3 |ref=Nakamura 2020}}

These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字). == See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]

{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢|1=Nobi}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 6</ref>. [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese law]] [[Category:11]][[ja:氏姓制度]] An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字). == See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]
== References == {{reflist|20em}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Naoki, Kōjirō |title=Ancient Japanese Clans and the Emperor |publisher=[[Hanawa Shobō]] |date=1964 |isbn=978-4-8273-1012-2 |ref=Naoki 1964}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |title=The Ancient Japanese Clan and Title System |publisher=[[Yagi Shoten]] |date=2009 |isbn=978-4-8406-2036-9 |ref=Nakamura 2009}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |editor=Sato, Makoto |title=Themes in Japanese Ancient History: Politics and Diplomacy |publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]] |date=2020 |isbn=978-4-642-08384-3 |ref=Nakamura 2020}}

{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権}}). The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字). == See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族|12=draft}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族|12=draft}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏|12=draft}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓|12=draft}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷|12=draft}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権|12=draft}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲|12=draft}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新|12=draft}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家|12=draft}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制|12=draft}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民|12=draft}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|12=draft}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族|12=draft}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢|1=Nobi|12=draft}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権|12=draft}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964|12=draft}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権|12=draft}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=draft}}, p. 6</ref>.
An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020|12=draft}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021|12=draft}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=draft}}, p. 24</ref>.
Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏|12=draft}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=draft}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏|12=draft}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣|12=draft}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連|12=draft}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造|12=draft}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏|12=draft}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏|12=draft}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏|12=draft}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏|12=draft}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=draft}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|12=draft}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|12=draft}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|12=draft}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字). == See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=draft}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]
== References == {{reflist|20em}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Naoki, Kōjirō |title=Ancient Japanese Clans and the Emperor |publisher=[[Hanawa Shobō]] |date=1964 |isbn=978-4-8273-1012-2 |ref=Naoki 1964}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |title=The Ancient Japanese Clan and Title System |publisher=[[Yagi Shoten]] |date=2009 |isbn=978-4-8406-2036-9 |ref=Nakamura 2009}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |editor=Sato, Makoto |title=Themes in Japanese Ancient History: Politics and Diplomacy |publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]] |date=2020 |isbn=978-4-642-08384-3 |ref=Nakamura 2020}}
<!--Uji-Kabane system--> <!--Uji-Kabane system-->

The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権|12=draft}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=draft}}, p. 6</ref>.
An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020|12=draft}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021|12=draft}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=draft}}, p. 24</ref>. Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏|12=draft}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=draft}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏|12=draft}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣|12=draft}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連|12=draft}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造|12=draft}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏|12=draft}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏|12=draft}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏|12=draft}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏|12=draft}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=draft}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|12=draft}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|12=draft}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|12=draft}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字). == See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=draft}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]
== References == {{reflist|20em}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Naoki, Kōjirō |title=Ancient Japanese Clans and the Emperor |publisher=[[Hanawa Shobō]] |date=1964 |isbn=978-4-8273-1012-2 |ref=Naoki 1964}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |title=The Ancient Japanese Clan and Title System |publisher=[[Yagi Shoten]] |date=2009 |isbn=978-4-8406-2036-9 |ref=Nakamura 2009}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |editor=Sato, Makoto |title=Themes in Japanese Ancient History: Politics and Diplomacy |publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]] |date=2020 |isbn=978-4-642-08384-3 |ref=Nakamura 2020}}

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=2018年3月}} '''氏姓制度'''（しせいせいど）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]において、中央[[貴族]]、ついで地方[[豪族]]が、[[国家]]（[[ヤマト王権]]）に対する貢献度、[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]政治上に占める地位に応じて、[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]より[[氏|氏（ウヂ）]]の名と[[カバネ|姓（カバネ）]]の名とを授与され、その特権的地位を[[世襲]]した制度。「'''氏姓の制'''（ウヂ・カバネのせい）」ともいい、「氏・姓」を音読して「'''氏姓'''（しせい）」ともいう。
[[大化の改新]]ののち、律令国家の形成に及ぶと、[[古代の戸籍制度|戸籍制]]によって、氏姓はかつての[[部民制|部民]]（べみん）、つまり一般民衆にまで拡大され、すべての階層の国家身分を表示するものとなった。氏姓を有しない者は、[[天皇]]・[[皇族]]と[[奴婢]]（ぬひ）のみとなった。
== 氏姓 == [[ヤマト王権]]においては全体を統合する大王の下で有力豪族たちが[[氏]]（ウヂ）として奉仕し王権を構成した。古代における氏（ウヂ）とはそれを束ねる有力な血縁集団の家系を中心として、その周縁に血縁・非血縁の様々な家が含まれる同族団あるいはその連合体である<ref name="直木1964p111">[[#直木 1964|直木 1964]], p. 111</ref>。この同族団の構成員は（特に中心的な家系において）実際に血縁関係にある場合が多いが例外を含み、また氏内部において身分差を内包する。また、氏の中心的な家系はヤマト王権と何らかの政治的関係を有するのが原則であり、ヤマト王権との関係によってもたらされる政治的権力が氏内部の統制と外部への拡大に重要な意義を持った<ref name="直木1964p111"/><ref name="中村2009p6">[[#中村 2009|中村 2009]], p. 6</ref>。このため日本古代のウヂは単なる自然発生的な血族集団としての[[氏族]]（Clan）とは異なり、ヤマト王権自体と密接に結びついて成立していた政治的集団または政治的組織であるとされている<ref name="中村2020p26">[[#中村 2020|中村 2020]], p. 26</ref><ref name="佐藤2021p8">[[#佐藤 2021|佐藤 2021]], p. 8</ref>。氏の成立が自然発生的なものでなく政治的関係性によるものであることは、氏名がしばしば仕奉すべき職掌を表し（つまりは天皇と氏の間の君臣関係を前提とし）、氏姓が制度的に定まった後も王権側が氏姓を賜与・変更する権能を保持したことにも表れている<ref name="中村2009p24">[[#中村 2009|中村 2009]], p. 24</ref>。
氏名（ウジの名前）は地名によるもの（[[蘇我氏]]、[[葛城氏]]、[[吉備氏]]、[[上毛野氏]]など）と職掌によるもの（[[物部氏]]、[[大伴氏]]、[[中臣氏]]など）に大別され、[[臣]]（オミ）、[[連]]（ムラジ）、[[造]]（ミヤツコ）などのような姓（[[カバネ]]）を帯びた。こうした氏姓（ウヂとカバネ）を持つことはヤマト王権の政事（マツリゴト）に何らかの形で関与していることを示していた<ref name="中村2020p26"/>。
== 氏姓制度の成立 == {{See|氏|カバネ}} 原始共同体においては、[[氏族]]や[[部族]]が社会の単位となった。
氏姓制度の成立時期は、5 - [[6世紀]]をさかのぼらない。同族のなかの特定の者が、[[臣]]、[[連]]、[[伴造]]、[[国造]]、[[百八十部]]（ももあまりやそのとも）、[[県主]]などの地位をあたえられ、それに応ずる氏姓を賜ったところに特色がある。各姓は以下のごとくである。
; [[臣]]（おみ） :[[葛城氏]]、[[平群氏]]、[[巨勢氏]]、[[春日氏]]、[[蘇我氏]]のように、ヤマト（奈良盆地周辺）の地名を氏の名とし、かつては[[大王 (ヤマト王権)|大王]]家と並ぶ立場にあり、ヤマト王権においても最高の地位を占めた豪族である。 ; [[連]]（むらじ） :[[大伴氏]]、[[物部氏]]、[[中臣氏]]、[[忌部氏]]、[[土師氏]]のように、ヤマト王権での職務を氏の名とし、大王家に従属する[[官人]]としての立場にあり、ヤマト王権の成立に重要な役割をはたした豪族である。 ; [[伴造]]（とものみやつこ） :連とも重なり合うが、おもにそのもとでヤマト王権の各部司を分掌した[[豪族]]である。[[弓削氏]]、[[矢集氏]]（やずめ）、[[服部氏]]、[[犬養氏]]（いぬかい）、[[舂米氏]]（つきしね）、[[倭文氏]]（しとり）などの氏や[[秦氏]]、[[東漢氏]]、[[西文氏]]（かわちのふみ）などの代表的な帰化人達に与えられた氏がある。連、造（みやつこ）、直（あたい）、公（きみ）などの姓を称した。 ; [[百八十部]]（ももあまりやそのとも） :さらにその下位にあり、部（べ）を直接に指揮する多くの伴（とも）をさす。首（おびと）、史（ふひと）、村主（すくり）、勝（すくり）などの姓（カバネ）を称した。 ; [[国造]]（くにのみやつこ） :代表的な地方豪族をさし、一面ではヤマト王権の地方官に組みこまれ、また在地の部民を率いる地方的伴造の地位にある者もあった。国造には、君（きみ）、直（あたい）の姓が多く、中には臣（おみ）を称するものもあった。 ; [[県主]]（あがたぬし） :これより古く、かつ小範囲の族長をさすものと思われる。いずれも地名を氏の名とする。
このように、氏姓制度とは、連―伴造―伴（百八十部）という、大王のもとでヤマト王権を構成し、職務を分掌し世襲する、いわゆる「負名氏」（なおいのうじ）を主体として生まれた。そののち、臣のように、元々は大王とならぶ地位にあった豪族にも及んだ。
===部民制=== {{See|部民制}} 氏姓は元来はヤマト王権を構成する臣・連・伴造・国造などの支配階級が称したものである（大王とその一族を除く）。しかし、6世紀には一般の民にも及んだ。これらの一般の民は、朝廷すなわち、大王、[[皇后|大后]]（おおきさき）などの后妃、[[皇子]]らの宮、さらに臣、連らの豪族に領有・支配されていた。そのため、一般の民の中から、朝廷に出仕して、職務の名を負う[[品部]]（しなべ）、大王名、宮号を負う[[名代]]・[[子代]]、[[屯倉]]の耕作民である[[田部]]などが必然的に生まれた。彼らは先進的な部民共同体の中で戸を単位に編成され、6世紀には籍帳に登載されて、正式に氏姓をもった。
これに対し、地方豪族の支配下にあった[[部曲|民部]]（かきべ）は、在地の族長を介して、共同体のまま部（べ）に編入し、族長をへて貢納させる形のものが多かった。そのため、地方豪族の支配下にあった一般の民にまで6世紀の段階で氏姓が及んでいたかどうかは定かではない。
== 律令国家による再編 == [[大化の改新]]により、氏姓制度による臣・連・伴造・国造を[[律令国家]]の官僚に再編し、[[部民]]を[[公民]]として、一律に国家のもとに帰属させた。
=== 甲子の宣 === [[664年]]（[[天智天皇]]3年）に、「甲子（かつし）の宣」が発せられた。これは、大化以来の[[官位]]を改め、大氏（おおうじ）、小氏（こうじ）、伴造氏（とものみやつこうじ）を定め、それぞれの[[氏上]]（うじのかみ）と、それに属する氏人（うじびと）の範囲を明確にしようとするものであった。つまり、官位の改定によって、大錦位（大氏）・小錦位（小氏）、つまり律令の四、五位以上に位置づけられる氏上をもつ氏を定めたものであり、これによって朝廷内の官位制度と全国の氏姓制度とを連動させようとした。さらにこのような氏上に属する氏人を父系による直系親族に限ることとし、従来の父系あるいは母系の原理による漠然とした氏の範囲を限定することとした。これにより、物部弓削（もののべゆげ）、阿倍布勢（あべのふせ）、蘇我石川（そがのいしかわ）などの複姓は、これ以後原則として消滅することとなる。
=== 八色の姓 === [[684年]]（[[天武天皇]]13年）に、「[[八色の姓]]」が制定された。その目的は、上位の4姓、つまり[[真人]]、[[朝臣]]、[[宿禰]]、[[忌寸]]を定めることである。真人は、[[継体天皇]]より数えて5世以内の世代の氏に与えられたといわれ、皇子・諸王につぐ皇親氏族を特定したので、[[飛鳥浄御原令]]で官位を皇子・諸王と貴族（諸臣）とで区別したことと共通する。したがって、貴族の姓としては、朝臣、宿禰、忌寸の3つである。以上が「甲子の宣」の大氏、小氏、伴造氏の発展形であり、その間にさらに氏族の再編が進められ、朝臣52氏、宿禰50氏、忌寸11氏に収められた。
[[大宝律令|大宝令]]（[[701年]]）で、貴族の三位以上と四、五位の官位にともなう特権が明確にされた。これに対応する氏姓も一応完成された。地方豪族についても、[[702年]]（[[大宝 (日本)|大宝]]2年）、諸国国造の氏姓を政府に登録することによって、中央豪族と同様の対応がなされたものとされる。
=== 公民の扱いと浸透 === 一般の[[公民]]については、[[670年]]（天智天皇9年）の[[古代の戸籍制度|庚午年籍]]、[[690年]]（[[持統天皇]]4年）の[[古代の戸籍制度|庚寅年籍]]によって、すべて戸籍に登載されることとなり、部姓を主とする氏姓制度が完成されることとなった。しかしながら、現存する[[702年]]の大宝二年籍に、氏姓を記入されていない者、国造族、県主族などと記された者がかなり存在するため、このとき、まだ無姓の者、族姓の者が多数いたことが窺える。
[[757年]]（[[天平宝字]]元年）、戸籍に無姓の者と族姓の者とをそのまま記すことをやめることとした。これは地方豪族の配下の[[百姓]]には、
# 所属が定まらず無姓のままの者、 # 国造、県主の共同体に属することを示すことによって族姓を仮称させた者、 # 姓を与えられていない新しい帰化人
が存在していたことを示している。そして、これ以後、このような者たちには正式に氏姓が与えられるようになった。
8 - 9世紀において改賜姓がさかんに行われているのは、八色の姓において、上級の氏姓にもれた下級の身分の者や、これらの農民を主な対象としたものである。その順位は、無姓を下級とし、造、公、史、勝、村主、拘登（ひと）、連と身分が上がっていく。これは、[[天武天皇|天武朝]]において氏上に相当する氏が八色の姓に改姓する前段階として、まず連への改姓が行われ、この連＝小錦位以上を基点として、忌寸以上の4つの姓へ改められたことと同様の対応である。
氏上である[[忌寸]]以上についても、補足的な氏姓の変更が行われている。氏の名において春日より大春日、中臣より大中臣への変更、また宿禰から大宿禰への変更が行われるなどしたため、氏姓の制は、全般的に、より緻密に浸透することになった。
これらの全般的な特徴として、まず首位の昇叙があり、ついでそれに連なる直系親族のみに対し氏姓の変更が行われるといった順序により同族の中から有力な者が抽出されるという点にある。この改賜姓を認可する権限は天皇にあった。
== 氏姓制の崩壊 == [[9世紀]]に、[[摂関政治]]により[[藤原北家|藤原朝臣]]が最も有力となった。また、[[桓武天皇]]より[[平氏|平朝臣]]、[[清和天皇]]などから[[源氏|源朝臣]]の氏姓（ウヂ・カバネ）が生まれたように、諸[[皇子]]に氏姓をあたえる[[臣籍降下]]が盛んに行われるようになった。これらのため、律令的氏姓制度は、人材登用制度としてはほとんど有効に機能しなくなった。
一方、律令的戸籍制度も次第に行われなくなり、[[10世紀]]には、地方[[豪族]]で実力を蓄えた者は、有力な[[貴族]]の[[家人]]となり、その氏姓を侵すようにさえなり、いわゆる[[冒名仮蔭]]（ぼうめいかいん）の現象が一般化した。そのため、天下の氏姓は、[[源氏|源]]・[[平氏|平]]・[[藤原氏|藤]]・[[橘氏|橘]]か、[[紀氏|紀]]、[[菅原氏|菅原]]、[[大江氏|大江]]、[[中原氏|中原]]、[[坂上氏|坂上]]、[[賀茂朝臣氏|賀茂]]、[[小野氏|小野]]、[[惟宗氏|惟宗]]、[[清原氏|清原]]などに集中されるようになった。これは家業の成立によって、特定の家柄が固定されるようになったためでもある。たとえば、[[越前国|越前]]の[[敦賀氏]]、[[熱田大宮司家]]らが[[藤原氏]]から養子を迎えて「藤原朝臣」を名乗ったり、それらの氏の女子をめとり母系によって「藤原朝臣」その他の氏姓を称した例もある。[[武士]]もまた、[[地頭]]として、[[本家]]、[[領家]]の氏姓を侵し、同じ氏姓を名乗る者が増えた。ここにおいて、同姓の間でも、さらに族名を分かつ必要にせまられ、[[貴族]]では家名、[[武士]]では[[名字]]が生ずるのである。
== 字（あざな）・苗字・名字 == 一方、氏姓のほかに、同時に発達したのが[[字|字（あざな）]]である。[[仮名 (通称)|仮名]]（けみよう）、[[呼名]]（よびな）ともいわれ、一種の私称であった。すでに『[[日本現報善悪霊異記|日本霊異記]]』に、[[紀伊国]]伊刀郡人文忌寸（ふみのいみき）を、上田三郎と称した例がある。上田は、伊刀郡上田邑の地名、三郎は三男の意である。
氏姓に取って代わることになる[[苗字]]（名字）は、このように字の一部分として発生し、さらに字から分離独立したものとされる。<!--現在のところ、「苗字」より「名字」の名称のほうが記録が古い。-->苗字は[[12世紀]]以後、氏姓と同じように用いられているが、初期の苗字は、居住地や領地の所有を表すために土地名を自分の名にしたため、所領が分かれたり別の土地の所領に移るなどして父子兄弟が苗字を異にしている場合が多かった。特に中世では戦乱や[[転封]]などにより所領が目まぐるしく変わり、苗字の持つ一族を表す機能は薄かった。しかし戦乱が減り身分や住居が安定してくると、苗字が家名・一族の名前を意味するようになり、他国に移っても一族の苗字は変更されないようになった。
今日的な意味での[[姓]]（セイ）の特徴は、基本的にはこの苗字（名字）から発生している。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 出典 === {{reflist|20em}}

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[氏姓制度]] to [[Uji-Kabane system]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=2018年3月}} '''氏姓制度'''（しせいせいど）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]において、中央[[貴族]]、ついで地方[[豪族]]が、[[国家]]（[[ヤマト王権]]）に対する貢献度、[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]政治上に占める地位に応じて、[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]より[[氏|氏（ウヂ）]]の名と[[カバネ|姓（カバネ）]]の名とを授与され、その特権的地位を[[世襲]]した制度。「'''氏姓の制'''（ウヂ・カバネのせい）」ともいい、「氏・姓」を音読して「'''氏姓'''（しせい）」ともいう。
[[大化の改新]]ののち、律令国家の形成に及ぶと、[[古代の戸籍制度|戸籍制]]によって、氏姓はかつての[[部民制|部民]]（べみん）、つまり一般民衆にまで拡大され、すべての階層の国家身分を表示するものとなった。氏姓を有しない者は、[[天皇]]・[[皇族]]と[[奴婢]]（ぬひ）のみとなった。
== 氏姓 == [[ヤマト王権]]においては全体を統合する大王の下で有力豪族たちが[[氏]]（ウヂ）として奉仕し王権を構成した。古代における氏（ウヂ）とはそれを束ねる有力な血縁集団の家系を中心として、その周縁に血縁・非血縁の様々な家が含まれる同族団あるいはその連合体である<ref name="直木1964p111">[[#直木 1964|直木 1964]], p. 111</ref>。この同族団の構成員は（特に中心的な家系において）実際に血縁関係にある場合が多いが例外を含み、また氏内部において身分差を内包する。また、氏の中心的な家系はヤマト王権と何らかの政治的関係を有するのが原則であり、ヤマト王権との関係によってもたらされる政治的権力が氏内部の統制と外部への拡大に重要な意義を持った<ref name="直木1964p111"/><ref name="中村2009p6">[[#中村 2009|中村 2009]], p. 6</ref>。このため日本古代のウヂは単なる自然発生的な血族集団としての[[氏族]]（Clan）とは異なり、ヤマト王権自体と密接に結びついて成立していた政治的集団または政治的組織であるとされている<ref name="中村2020p26">[[#中村 2020|中村 2020]], p. 26</ref><ref name="佐藤2021p8">[[#佐藤 2021|佐藤 2021]], p. 8</ref>。氏の成立が自然発生的なものでなく政治的関係性によるものであることは、氏名がしばしば仕奉すべき職掌を表し（つまりは天皇と氏の間の君臣関係を前提とし）、氏姓が制度的に定まった後も王権側が氏姓を賜与・変更する権能を保持したことにも表れている<ref name="中村2009p24">[[#中村 2009|中村 2009]], p. 24</ref>。
氏名（ウジの名前）は地名によるもの（[[蘇我氏]]、[[葛城氏]]、[[吉備氏]]、[[上毛野氏]]など）と職掌によるもの（[[物部氏]]、[[大伴氏]]、[[中臣氏]]など）に大別され、[[臣]]（オミ）、[[連]]（ムラジ）、[[造]]（ミヤツコ）などのような姓（[[カバネ]]）を帯びた。こうした氏姓（ウヂとカバネ）を持つことはヤマト王権の政事（マツリゴト）に何らかの形で関与していることを示していた<ref name="中村2020p26"/>。
== 氏姓制度の成立 == {{See|氏|カバネ}} 原始共同体においては、[[氏族]]や[[部族]]が社会の単位となった。
氏姓制度の成立時期は、5 - [[6世紀]]をさかのぼらない。同族のなかの特定の者が、[[臣]]、[[連]]、[[伴造]]、[[国造]]、[[百八十部]]（ももあまりやそのとも）、[[県主]]などの地位をあたえられ、それに応ずる氏姓を賜ったところに特色がある。各姓は以下のごとくである。
; [[臣]]（おみ） :[[葛城氏]]、[[平群氏]]、[[巨勢氏]]、[[春日氏]]、[[蘇我氏]]のように、ヤマト（奈良盆地周辺）の地名を氏の名とし、かつては[[大王 (ヤマト王権)|大王]]家と並ぶ立場にあり、ヤマト王権においても最高の地位を占めた豪族である。 ; [[連]]（むらじ） :[[大伴氏]]、[[物部氏]]、[[中臣氏]]、[[忌部氏]]、[[土師氏]]のように、ヤマト王権での職務を氏の名とし、大王家に従属する[[官人]]としての立場にあり、ヤマト王権の成立に重要な役割をはたした豪族である。 ; [[伴造]]（とものみやつこ） :連とも重なり合うが、おもにそのもとでヤマト王権の各部司を分掌した[[豪族]]である。[[弓削氏]]、[[矢集氏]]（やずめ）、[[服部氏]]、[[犬養氏]]（いぬかい）、[[舂米氏]]（つきしね）、[[倭文氏]]（しとり）などの氏や[[秦氏]]、[[東漢氏]]、[[西文氏]]（かわちのふみ）などの代表的な帰化人達に与えられた氏がある。連、造（みやつこ）、直（あたい）、公（きみ）などの姓を称した。 ; [[百八十部]]（ももあまりやそのとも） :さらにその下位にあり、部（べ）を直接に指揮する多くの伴（とも）をさす。首（おびと）、史（ふひと）、村主（すくり）、勝（すくり）などの姓（カバネ）を称した。 ; [[国造]]（くにのみやつこ） :代表的な地方豪族をさし、一面ではヤマト王権の地方官に組みこまれ、また在地の部民を率いる地方的伴造の地位にある者もあった。国造には、君（きみ）、直（あたい）の姓が多く、中には臣（おみ）を称するものもあった。 ; [[県主]]（あがたぬし） :これより古く、かつ小範囲の族長をさすものと思われる。いずれも地名を氏の名とする。
このように、氏姓制度とは、連―伴造―伴（百八十部）という、大王のもとでヤマト王権を構成し、職務を分掌し世襲する、いわゆる「負名氏」（なおいのうじ）を主体として生まれた。そののち、臣のように、元々は大王とならぶ地位にあった豪族にも及んだ。
===部民制=== {{See|部民制}} 氏姓は元来はヤマト王権を構成する臣・連・伴造・国造などの支配階級が称したものである（大王とその一族を除く）。しかし、6世紀には一般の民にも及んだ。これらの一般の民は、朝廷すなわち、大王、[[皇后|大后]]（おおきさき）などの后妃、[[皇子]]らの宮、さらに臣、連らの豪族に領有・支配されていた。そのため、一般の民の中から、朝廷に出仕して、職務の名を負う[[品部]]（しなべ）、大王名、宮号を負う[[名代]]・[[子代]]、[[屯倉]]の耕作民である[[田部]]などが必然的に生まれた。彼らは先進的な部民共同体の中で戸を単位に編成され、6世紀には籍帳に登載されて、正式に氏姓をもった。
これに対し、地方豪族の支配下にあった[[部曲|民部]]（かきべ）は、在地の族長を介して、共同体のまま部（べ）に編入し、族長をへて貢納させる形のものが多かった。そのため、地方豪族の支配下にあった一般の民にまで6世紀の段階で氏姓が及んでいたかどうかは定かではない。
== 律令国家による再編 == [[大化の改新]]により、氏姓制度による臣・連・伴造・国造を[[律令国家]]の官僚に再編し、[[部民]]を[[公民]]として、一律に国家のもとに帰属させた。
=== 甲子の宣 === [[664年]]（[[天智天皇]]3年）に、「甲子（かつし）の宣」が発せられた。これは、大化以来の[[官位]]を改め、大氏（おおうじ）、小氏（こうじ）、伴造氏（とものみやつこうじ）を定め、それぞれの[[氏上]]（うじのかみ）と、それに属する氏人（うじびと）の範囲を明確にしようとするものであった。つまり、官位の改定によって、大錦位（大氏）・小錦位（小氏）、つまり律令の四、五位以上に位置づけられる氏上をもつ氏を定めたものであり、これによって朝廷内の官位制度と全国の氏姓制度とを連動させようとした。さらにこのような氏上に属する氏人を父系による直系親族に限ることとし、従来の父系あるいは母系の原理による漠然とした氏の範囲を限定することとした。これにより、物部弓削（もののべゆげ）、阿倍布勢（あべのふせ）、蘇我石川（そがのいしかわ）などの複姓は、これ以後原則として消滅することとなる。
=== 八色の姓 === [[684年]]（[[天武天皇]]13年）に、「[[八色の姓]]」が制定された。その目的は、上位の4姓、つまり[[真人]]、[[朝臣]]、[[宿禰]]、[[忌寸]]を定めることである。真人は、[[継体天皇]]より数えて5世以内の世代の氏に与えられたといわれ、皇子・諸王につぐ皇親氏族を特定したので、[[飛鳥浄御原令]]で官位を皇子・諸王と貴族（諸臣）とで区別したことと共通する。したがって、貴族の姓としては、朝臣、宿禰、忌寸の3つである。以上が「甲子の宣」の大氏、小氏、伴造氏の発展形であり、その間にさらに氏族の再編が進められ、朝臣52氏、宿禰50氏、忌寸11氏に収められた。
[[大宝律令|大宝令]]（[[701年]]）で、貴族の三位以上と四、五位の官位にともなう特権が明確にされた。これに対応する氏姓も一応完成された。地方豪族についても、[[702年]]（[[大宝 (日本)|大宝]]2年）、諸国国造の氏姓を政府に登録することによって、中央豪族と同様の対応がなされたものとされる。
=== 公民の扱いと浸透 === 一般の[[公民]]については、[[670年]]（天智天皇9年）の[[古代の戸籍制度|庚午年籍]]、[[690年]]（[[持統天皇]]4年）の[[古代の戸籍制度|庚寅年籍]]によって、すべて戸籍に登載されることとなり、部姓を主とする氏姓制度が完成されることとなった。しかしながら、現存する[[702年]]の大宝二年籍に、氏姓を記入されていない者、国造族、県主族などと記された者がかなり存在するため、このとき、まだ無姓の者、族姓の者が多数いたことが窺える。
[[757年]]（[[天平宝字]]元年）、戸籍に無姓の者と族姓の者とをそのまま記すことをやめることとした。これは地方豪族の配下の[[百姓]]には、
# 所属が定まらず無姓のままの者、 # 国造、県主の共同体に属することを示すことによって族姓を仮称させた者、 # 姓を与えられていない新しい帰化人
が存在していたことを示している。そして、これ以後、このような者たちには正式に氏姓が与えられるようになった。
8 - 9世紀において改賜姓がさかんに行われているのは、八色の姓において、上級の氏姓にもれた下級の身分の者や、これらの農民を主な対象としたものである。その順位は、無姓を下級とし、造、公、史、勝、村主、拘登（ひと）、連と身分が上がっていく。これは、[[天武天皇|天武朝]]において氏上に相当する氏が八色の姓に改姓する前段階として、まず連への改姓が行われ、この連＝小錦位以上を基点として、忌寸以上の4つの姓へ改められたことと同様の対応である。
氏上である[[忌寸]]以上についても、補足的な氏姓の変更が行われている。氏の名において春日より大春日、中臣より大中臣への変更、また宿禰から大宿禰への変更が行われるなどしたため、氏姓の制は、全般的に、より緻密に浸透することになった。
これらの全般的な特徴として、まず首位の昇叙があり、ついでそれに連なる直系親族のみに対し氏姓の変更が行われるといった順序により同族の中から有力な者が抽出されるという点にある。この改賜姓を認可する権限は天皇にあった。
== 氏姓制の崩壊 == [[9世紀]]に、[[摂関政治]]により[[藤原北家|藤原朝臣]]が最も有力となった。また、[[桓武天皇]]より[[平氏|平朝臣]]、[[清和天皇]]などから[[源氏|源朝臣]]の氏姓（ウヂ・カバネ）が生まれたように、諸[[皇子]]に氏姓をあたえる[[臣籍降下]]が盛んに行われるようになった。これらのため、律令的氏姓制度は、人材登用制度としてはほとんど有効に機能しなくなった。
一方、律令的戸籍制度も次第に行われなくなり、[[10世紀]]には、地方[[豪族]]で実力を蓄えた者は、有力な[[貴族]]の[[家人]]となり、その氏姓を侵すようにさえなり、いわゆる[[冒名仮蔭]]（ぼうめいかいん）の現象が一般化した。そのため、天下の氏姓は、[[源氏|源]]・[[平氏|平]]・[[藤原氏|藤]]・[[橘氏|橘]]か、[[紀氏|紀]]、[[菅原氏|菅原]]、[[大江氏|大江]]、[[中原氏|中原]]、[[坂上氏|坂上]]、[[賀茂朝臣氏|賀茂]]、[[小野氏|小野]]、[[惟宗氏|惟宗]]、[[清原氏|清原]]などに集中されるようになった。これは家業の成立によって、特定の家柄が固定されるようになったためでもある。たとえば、[[越前国|越前]]の[[敦賀氏]]、[[熱田大宮司家]]らが[[藤原氏]]から養子を迎えて「藤原朝臣」を名乗ったり、それらの氏の女子をめとり母系によって「藤原朝臣」その他の氏姓を称した例もある。[[武士]]もまた、[[地頭]]として、[[本家]]、[[領家]]の氏姓を侵し、同じ氏姓を名乗る者が増えた。ここにおいて、同姓の間でも、さらに族名を分かつ必要にせまられ、[[貴族]]では家名、[[武士]]では[[名字]]が生ずるのである。
== 字（あざな）・苗字・名字 == 一方、氏姓のほかに、同時に発達したのが[[字|字（あざな）]]である。[[仮名 (通称)|仮名]]（けみよう）、[[呼名]]（よびな）ともいわれ、一種の私称であった。すでに『[[日本現報善悪霊異記|日本霊異記]]』に、[[紀伊国]]伊刀郡人文忌寸（ふみのいみき）を、上田三郎と称した例がある。上田は、伊刀郡上田邑の地名、三郎は三男の意である。
氏姓に取って代わることになる[[苗字]]（名字）は、このように字の一部分として発生し、さらに字から分離独立したものとされる。<!--現在のところ、「苗字」より「名字」の名称のほうが記録が古い。-->苗字は[[12世紀]]以後、氏姓と同じように用いられているが、初期の苗字は、居住地や領地の所有を表すために土地名を自分の名にしたため、所領が分かれたり別の土地の所領に移るなどして父子兄弟が苗字を異にしている場合が多かった。特に中世では戦乱や[[転封]]などにより所領が目まぐるしく変わり、苗字の持つ一族を表す機能は薄かった。しかし戦乱が減り身分や住居が安定してくると、苗字が家名・一族の名前を意味するようになり、他国に移っても一族の苗字は変更されないようになった。
今日的な意味での[[姓]]（セイ）の特徴は、基本的にはこの苗字（名字）から発生している。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 出典 === {{reflist|20em}}

{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏|1=Uji (clan)|lt=Uji|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制|qq=draft|qq=draft}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢|1=Nobi|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, p. 6</ref>.
An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, p. 24</ref>. Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their Rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into Ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字). == See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]
== References == {{reflist|20em}}

This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, p. 24</ref>. Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their Rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into Ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字). == See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]
== References == {{reflist|20em}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Naoki, Kōjirō |title=Ancient Japanese Clans and the Emperor |publisher=[[Hanawa Shobō]] |date=1964 |isbn=978-4-8273-1012-2 |ref=Naoki 1964}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |title=The Ancient Japanese Clan and Title System |publisher=[[Yagi Shoten]] |date=2009 |isbn=978-4-8406-2036-9 |ref=Nakamura 2009}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |editor=Sato, Makoto |title=Themes in Japanese Ancient History: Politics and Diplomacy |publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]] |date=2020 |isbn=978-4-642-08384-3 |ref=Nakamura 2020}}

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} '''Shisei System''' (氏姓制度) refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|aristocrats|ja|貴族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/aristocrats|qq=}} and later local {{ill|feudal lords|ja|豪族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/feudal lords|qq=}} were granted the names of {{ill|uji|ja|氏|1=Uji (clan)|lt=Uji|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/uji|13=User:Immanuelle/Uji (clan)|qq=}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabane|qq=}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court of Japan|qq=}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato court|qq=}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|hereditary|ja|世襲|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hereditary|qq=}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reforms|ja|大化の改新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taika Reforms|qq=}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient Japanese household registration system|qq=}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bemmin|qq=}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor of Japan|qq=}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Family (Japan)|qq=}}, and {{ill|slaves|ja|奴婢|1=Nobi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/slaves|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobi|qq=}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato court|qq=}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naoki 1964|qq=}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato court|ja|ヤマト王権|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato court|qq=}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2009|qq=}}, p. 6</ref>.
An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2020|qq=}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato 2021|qq=}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2009|qq=}}, p. 24</ref>. Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga clan|qq=}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuragi clan|qq=}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibi clan|qq=}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsuke clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe clan|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo clan|qq=}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi clan|qq=}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi|ja|臣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi|qq=}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|ja|連|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muraji|qq=}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyatsuko|qq=}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their Rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''[[Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|ja|蘇我氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga clan|qq=}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|ja|葛城氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuragi clan|qq=}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe clan|qq=}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo clan|qq=}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|ja|秦氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hata clan|qq=}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuge clan|qq=}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ranks of Titles|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into Ranks such as [[Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara clan|qq=}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|平氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan|qq=}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|ja|源氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto clan|qq=}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字). == See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]
== References == {{reflist|20em}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Naoki, Kōjirō |title=Ancient Japanese Clans and the Emperor |publisher=[[Hanawa Shobō]] |date=1964 |isbn=978-4-8273-1012-2 |ref=Naoki 1964}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |title=The Ancient Japanese Clan and Title System |publisher=[[Yagi Shoten]] |date=2009 |isbn=978-4-8406-2036-9 |ref=Nakamura 2009}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |editor=Sato, Makoto |title=Themes in Japanese Ancient History: Politics and Diplomacy |publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]] |date=2020 |isbn=978-4-642-08384-3 |ref=Nakamura 2020}}
