This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become [[:en:Line of hereditary succession|hereditary]]. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]], as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 律令制}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient Japanese household registration system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戸籍|1=family register|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q485228}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bemmin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2892644}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Family (Japan)|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}, and [[:en:Nobi|Nobi]].
== Uji and Kabane == In the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato court]], powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naoki 1964|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato court]], which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2009|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 6</ref>.
An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2020|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato 2021|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2009|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 24</ref>. Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., [[:en:Soga clan|Soga clan]], [[:en:Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi clan]], [[:en:Kibi clan|Kibi clan]], {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsuke clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}) or occupational roles (e.g., [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]], [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]]). These uji were associated with kabane such as [[:en:Omi (title)|Omi]] (omi), [[:en:Muraji|Muraji]] (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyatsuko|1=Miyatsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526942}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Clan and Title System == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The clan and title system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[:en:Omi|Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their Rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''[[:en:Omi|Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the [[:en:Soga clan|Soga clan]] and [[:en:Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi clan]] held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]] and [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]] supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the [[:en:Hata clan|Hata clan]] and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuge clan|qq=}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The clan and title system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ranks of Titles|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into Ranks such as [[:en:Mahito|Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Clan and Title System == By the 9th century, the dominance of the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]] and [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字). == See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[:en:Family name|Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]
== References == {{reflist|20em}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Naoki, Kōjirō |title=Ancient Japanese Clans and the Emperor |publisher=[[Hanawa Shobō]] |date=1964 |isbn=978-4-8273-1012-2 |ref=Naoki 1964}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |title=The Ancient Japanese Clan and Title System |publisher=[[Yagi Shoten]] |date=2009 |isbn=978-4-8406-2036-9 |ref=Nakamura 2009}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |editor=Sato, Makoto |title=Themes in Japanese Ancient History: Politics and Diplomacy |publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]] |date=2020 |isbn=978-4-642-08384-3 |ref=Nakamura 2020}}

<!--Uji-Kabane system--><!--Uji-Kabane system--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:씨성제도]] [[ru:Система удзи-кабанэ]] [[uk:Система удзі-кабане]]
{{wikidata link|Q4420989}} [[Category:Ancient Laws of Japan]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Japanese-language surnames]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏姓制度]][[ko:씨성제도]][[ru:Система удзи-кабанэ]][[uk:Система удзі-кабане]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏姓制度]][[ko:씨성제도]][[ru:Система удзи-кабанэ]][[uk:Система удзі-кабане]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏姓制度]][[ko:씨성제도]][[ru:Система удзи-кабанэ]][[uk:Система удзі-кабане]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q4420989]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} {{nihongo|'''Uji-Kabane system'''|氏姓制度||onyomi: Shisei}} refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central [[:en:Nobility|aristocrats]] and later local [[:en:Gōzoku|feudal lords]] were granted the names of [[:en:Uji (clan)|Uji]] (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabane|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (kabane) by the [[:en:Royal court|Imperial Court of Japan]] based on their contributions to the state (the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato court]]). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become [[:en:Line of hereditary succession|hereditary]]. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the [[:en:Taika Reform|Taika Reforms]], as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 律令制}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient Japanese household registration system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戸籍|1=family register|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q485228}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bemmin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2892644}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Family (Japan)|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}, and [[:en:Nobi|Nobi]].
== Uji and Kabane == In the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato court]], powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naoki 1964|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato court]], which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2009|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 6</ref>.
An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2020|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato 2021|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2009|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 24</ref>. Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., [[:en:Soga clan|Soga clan]], [[:en:Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi clan]], [[:en:Kibi clan|Kibi clan]], {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsuke clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}) or occupational roles (e.g., [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]], [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]]). These uji were associated with kabane such as [[:en:Omi (title)|Omi]] (omi), [[:en:Muraji|Muraji]] (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyatsuko|1=Miyatsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526942}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Uji-Kabane system == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The Uji-Kabane system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as [[:en:Omi|Omi]], [[Muraji]], [[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their Rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''[[:en:Omi|Omi]] (臣):''' Powerful clans like the [[:en:Soga clan|Soga clan]] and [[:en:Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi clan]] held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the [[:en:Mononobe clan|Mononobe clan]] and [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]] supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the [[:en:Hata clan|Hata clan]] and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuge clan|qq=}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The Uji-Kabane system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ranks of Titles|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into Ranks such as [[:en:Mahito|Mahito]] (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Uji-Kabane system == By the 9th century, the dominance of the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]] and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the [[:en:Taira clan|Taira clan]] and [[:en:Minamoto clan|Minamoto clan]], marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字). == See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[:en:Family name|Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]
== References == {{reflist|20em}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Naoki, Kōjirō |title=Ancient Japanese Clans and the Emperor |publisher=[[Hanawa Shobō]] |date=1964 |isbn=978-4-8273-1012-2 |ref=Naoki 1964}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |title=The Ancient Japanese Uji-Kabane system |publisher=[[Yagi Shoten]] |date=2009 |isbn=978-4-8406-2036-9 |ref=Nakamura 2009}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |editor=Sato, Makoto |title=Themes in Japanese Ancient History: Politics and Diplomacy |publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]] |date=2020 |isbn=978-4-642-08384-3 |ref=Nakamura 2020}}
{{translated page|ja|氏姓制度|version=100277379|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:氏姓制度]]

These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi (title)|en|Omi (title)|WD=Q2574718|lt=Omi}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|en|Muraji|WD=Q2735611|lt=Muraji}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyatsuko|1=Miyatsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526942}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Uji-Kabane system == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The Uji-Kabane system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as {{ill|Omi|en|Omi|WD=Q359551|lt=Omi}}, [[Muraji]], [[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their Rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''{{ill|Omi|en|Omi|WD=Q359551|lt=Omi}} (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|WD=Q909854|lt=Soga clan}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|en|Hata clan|WD=Q987290|lt=Hata clan}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuge clan|qq=}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The Uji-Kabane system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ranks of Titles|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into Ranks such as {{ill|Mahito|en|Mahito|WD=Q85846522|lt=Mahito}} (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Uji-Kabane system == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|en|Fujiwara clan|WD=Q641628|lt=Fujiwara clan}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字). == See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[:en:Family name|Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]
== References == {{reflist|20em}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Naoki, Kōjirō |title=Ancient Japanese Clans and the Emperor |publisher=[[Hanawa Shobō]] |date=1964 |isbn=978-4-8273-1012-2 |ref=Naoki 1964}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |title=The Ancient Japanese Uji-Kabane system |publisher=[[Yagi Shoten]] |date=2009 |isbn=978-4-8406-2036-9 |ref=Nakamura 2009}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |editor=Sato, Makoto |title=Themes in Japanese Ancient History: Politics and Diplomacy |publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]] |date=2020 |isbn=978-4-642-08384-3 |ref=Nakamura 2020}}

{{wikidata link|Q4420989}} [[Category:Ancient Laws of Japan]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Japanese surnames]] [[Category:Japanese-language surnames]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏姓制度]][[ko:씨성제도]][[ru:Система удзи-кабанэ]][[uk:Система удзі-кабане]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏姓制度]][[ko:씨성제도]][[ru:Система удзи-кабанэ]][[uk:Система удзі-кабане]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏姓制度]][[ko:씨성제도]][[ru:Система удзи-кабанэ]][[uk:Система удзі-кабане]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q4420989]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} {{nihongo|'''Uji-Kabane system'''|氏姓制度||onyomi: Shisei}} refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|Nobility|en|Nobility|WD=Q134737|lt=aristocrats}} and later local {{ill|Gōzoku|en|Gōzoku|WD=Q84066|lt=feudal lords}} were granted the names of {{ill|Uji (clan)|en|Uji (clan)|WD=Q2575910|lt=Uji}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabane|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court of Japan}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|Line of hereditary succession|en|Line of hereditary succession|WD=Q756689|lt=hereditary}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reforms}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 律令制}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient Japanese household registration system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戸籍|1=family register|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q485228}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bemmin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2892644}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Family (Japan)|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}, and {{ill|Nobi|en|Nobi|WD=Q1146808|lt=Nobi}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naoki 1964|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2009|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 6</ref>.
An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2020|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato 2021|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2009|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 24</ref>. Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|WD=Q909854|lt=Soga clan}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|en|Kibi clan|WD=Q11412645|lt=Kibi clan}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsuke clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|en|Nakatomi clan|WD=Q1198447|lt=Nakatomi clan}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi (title)|en|Omi (title)|WD=Q2574718|lt=Omi}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|en|Muraji|WD=Q2735611|lt=Muraji}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyatsuko|1=Miyatsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526942}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Uji-Kabane system == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The Uji-Kabane system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as {{ill|Omi|en|Omi|WD=Q359551|lt=Omi}}, [[Muraji]], [[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their Rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''{{ill|Omi|en|Omi|WD=Q359551|lt=Omi}} (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|WD=Q909854|lt=Soga clan}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|en|Hata clan|WD=Q987290|lt=Hata clan}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuge clan|qq=}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The Uji-Kabane system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ranks of Titles|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into Ranks such as {{ill|Mahito|en|Mahito|WD=Q85846522|lt=Mahito}} (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Uji-Kabane system == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|en|Fujiwara clan|WD=Q641628|lt=Fujiwara clan}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字). == See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[:en:Family name|Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]
== References == {{reflist|20em}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Naoki, Kōjirō |title=Ancient Japanese Clans and the Emperor |publisher=[[Hanawa Shobō]] |date=1964 |isbn=978-4-8273-1012-2 |ref=Naoki 1964}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |title=The Ancient Japanese Uji-Kabane system |publisher=[[Yagi Shoten]] |date=2009 |isbn=978-4-8406-2036-9 |ref=Nakamura 2009}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |editor=Sato, Makoto |title=Themes in Japanese Ancient History: Politics and Diplomacy |publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]] |date=2020 |isbn=978-4-642-08384-3 |ref=Nakamura 2020}}

After the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reforms}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 律令制}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient Japanese household registration system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戸籍|1=family register|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q485228}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bemmin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2892644}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Family (Japan)|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}, and {{ill|Nobi|en|Nobi|WD=Q1146808|lt=Nobi}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naoki 1964|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 111</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2009|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 24</ref>. Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|WD=Q909854|lt=Soga clan}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|en|Kibi clan|WD=Q11412645|lt=Kibi clan}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsuke clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|en|Nakatomi clan|WD=Q1198447|lt=Nakatomi clan}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi (title)|en|Omi (title)|WD=Q2574718|lt=Omi}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|en|Muraji|WD=Q2735611|lt=Muraji}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyatsuko|1=Miyatsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526942}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Uji-Kabane system == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The Uji-Kabane system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as {{ill|Omi|en|Omi|WD=Q359551|lt=Omi}}, [[Muraji]], [[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their Rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''{{ill|Omi|en|Omi|WD=Q359551|lt=Omi}} (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|WD=Q909854|lt=Soga clan}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|en|Hata clan|WD=Q987290|lt=Hata clan}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuge clan|qq=}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The Uji-Kabane system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.

This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ranks of Titles|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into Ranks such as {{ill|Mahito|en|Mahito|WD=Q85846522|lt=Mahito}} (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).

The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字). == See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[:en:Family name|Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏姓制度]][[ko:씨성제도]][[ru:Система удзи-кабанэ]][[uk:Система удзі-кабане]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏姓制度]][[ko:씨성제도]][[ru:Система удзи-кабанэ]][[uk:Система удзі-кабане]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏姓制度]][[ko:씨성제도]][[ru:Система удзи-кабанэ]][[uk:Система удзі-кабане]]
{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} {{nihongo|'''Uji-Kabane system'''|氏姓制度||onyomi: Shisei}} refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|Nobility|en|Nobility|WD=Q134737|lt=aristocrats}} and later local {{ill|Gōzoku|en|Gōzoku|WD=Q84066|lt=feudal lords}} were granted the names of {{ill|Uji (clan)|en|Uji (clan)|WD=Q2575910|lt=Uji}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabane|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court of Japan}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|Line of hereditary succession|en|Line of hereditary succession|WD=Q756689|lt=hereditary}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reforms}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 律令制}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient Japanese household registration system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戸籍|1=family register|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q485228}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bemmin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2892644}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Family (Japan)|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}, and {{ill|Nobi|en|Nobi|WD=Q1146808|lt=Nobi}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naoki 1964|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2009|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 6</ref>.
An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2020|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato 2021|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2009|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 24</ref>. Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|WD=Q909854|lt=Soga clan}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|en|Kibi clan|WD=Q11412645|lt=Kibi clan}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsuke clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|en|Nakatomi clan|WD=Q1198447|lt=Nakatomi clan}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi (title)|en|Omi (title)|WD=Q2574718|lt=Omi}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|en|Muraji|WD=Q2735611|lt=Muraji}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyatsuko|1=Miyatsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526942}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Uji-Kabane system == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The Uji-Kabane system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as {{ill|Omi|en|Omi|WD=Q359551|lt=Omi}}, [[Muraji]], [[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their Rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''{{ill|Omi|en|Omi|WD=Q359551|lt=Omi}} (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|WD=Q909854|lt=Soga clan}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|en|Hata clan|WD=Q987290|lt=Hata clan}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuge clan|qq=}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The Uji-Kabane system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ranks of Titles|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into Ranks such as {{ill|Mahito|en|Mahito|WD=Q85846522|lt=Mahito}} (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Uji-Kabane system == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|en|Fujiwara clan|WD=Q641628|lt=Fujiwara clan}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字). == See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[:en:Family name|Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]
== References == {{reflist|20em}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Naoki, Kōjirō |title=Ancient Japanese Clans and the Emperor |publisher=[[Hanawa Shobō]] |date=1964 |isbn=978-4-8273-1012-2 |ref=Naoki 1964}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |title=The Ancient Japanese Uji-Kabane system |publisher=[[Yagi Shoten]] |date=2009 |isbn=978-4-8406-2036-9 |ref=Nakamura 2009}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |editor=Sato, Makoto |title=Themes in Japanese Ancient History: Politics and Diplomacy |publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]] |date=2020 |isbn=978-4-642-08384-3 |ref=Nakamura 2020}}
{{translated page|ja|氏姓制度|version=100277379|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:氏姓制度]]

The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato Kingship}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2009|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 6</ref>.
An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2020|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato 2021|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2009|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 24</ref>. Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|WD=Q909854|lt=Soga clan|1=Soga clan}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan|1=Katsuragi clan}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|en|Kibi clan|WD=Q11412645|lt=Kibi clan|1=Kibi clan}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsuke clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan|1=Mononobe clan}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan|1=Ōtomo clan (ancient)}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|en|Nakatomi clan|WD=Q1198447|lt=Nakatomi clan|1=Nakatomi clan}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi (title)|en|Omi (title)|WD=Q2574718|lt=Omi|1=Omi (title)}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|en|Muraji|WD=Q2735611|lt=Muraji|1=Muraji}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyatsuko|1=Q55526942|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526942}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Uji-Kabane system == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The Uji-Kabane system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as {{ill|Omi|en|Omi|WD=Q359551|lt=Omi|1=Omi}}, [[Muraji]], [[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their Rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''{{ill|Omi|en|Omi|WD=Q359551|lt=Omi|1=Omi}} (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|WD=Q909854|lt=Soga clan|1=Soga clan}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan|1=Katsuragi clan}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan|1=Mononobe clan}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan|1=Ōtomo clan (ancient)}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|en|Hata clan|WD=Q987290|lt=Hata clan|1=Hata clan}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuge clan|qq=}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The Uji-Kabane system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ranks of Titles|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into Ranks such as {{ill|Mahito|en|Mahito|WD=Q85846522|lt=Mahito|1=Mahito}} (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Uji-Kabane system == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|en|Fujiwara clan|WD=Q641628|lt=Fujiwara clan|1=Fujiwara clan}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan|1=Taira clan}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan|1=Minamoto clan}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字). == See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[:en:Family name|Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏姓制度]][[ko:씨성제도]][[ru:Система удзи-кабанэ]][[uk:Система удзі-кабане]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏姓制度]][[ko:씨성제도]][[ru:Система удзи-кабанэ]][[uk:Система удзі-кабане]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏姓制度]][[ko:씨성제도]][[ru:Система удзи-кабанэ]][[uk:Система удзі-кабане]]
{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} {{nihongo|'''Uji-Kabane system'''|氏姓制度||onyomi: Shisei}} refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|Nobility|en|Nobility|WD=Q134737|lt=aristocrats|1=Nobility}} and later local {{ill|Gōzoku|en|Gōzoku|WD=Q84066|lt=feudal lords|1=Gōzoku}} were granted the names of {{ill|Uji (clan)|en|Uji (clan)|WD=Q2575910|lt=Uji|1=Uji (clan)}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabane|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court of Japan|1=Royal court}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato Kingship}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|Line of hereditary succession|en|Line of hereditary succession|WD=Q756689|lt=hereditary|1=Line of hereditary succession}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reforms|1=Taika Reform}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 律令制}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient Japanese household registration system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戸籍|1=Family register|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q485228}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bemmin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=Bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2892644}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Family (Japan)|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}, and {{ill|Nobi|en|Nobi|WD=Q1146808|lt=Nobi|1=Nobi}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato Kingship}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naoki 1964|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato Kingship}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2009|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 6</ref>.
An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2020|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato 2021|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2009|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 24</ref>. Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|WD=Q909854|lt=Soga clan|1=Soga clan}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan|1=Katsuragi clan}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|en|Kibi clan|WD=Q11412645|lt=Kibi clan|1=Kibi clan}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsuke clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan|1=Mononobe clan}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan|1=Ōtomo clan (ancient)}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|en|Nakatomi clan|WD=Q1198447|lt=Nakatomi clan|1=Nakatomi clan}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi (title)|en|Omi (title)|WD=Q2574718|lt=Omi|1=Omi (title)}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|en|Muraji|WD=Q2735611|lt=Muraji|1=Muraji}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyatsuko|1=Q55526942|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526942}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Uji-Kabane system == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The Uji-Kabane system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as {{ill|Omi|en|Omi|WD=Q359551|lt=Omi|1=Omi}}, [[Muraji]], [[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their Rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''{{ill|Omi|en|Omi|WD=Q359551|lt=Omi|1=Omi}} (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|WD=Q909854|lt=Soga clan|1=Soga clan}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan|1=Katsuragi clan}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan|1=Mononobe clan}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan|1=Ōtomo clan (ancient)}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|en|Hata clan|WD=Q987290|lt=Hata clan|1=Hata clan}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuge clan|qq=}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The Uji-Kabane system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ranks of Titles|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into Ranks such as {{ill|Mahito|en|Mahito|WD=Q85846522|lt=Mahito|1=Mahito}} (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Uji-Kabane system == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|en|Fujiwara clan|WD=Q641628|lt=Fujiwara clan|1=Fujiwara clan}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan|1=Taira clan}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan|1=Minamoto clan}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字). == See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[:en:Family name|Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]

{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} {{nihongo|'''Uji-Kabane system'''|氏姓制度||onyomi: Shisei}} refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|Nobility|en|Nobility|WD=Q134737|lt=aristocrats|1=Nobility}} and later local {{ill|Gōzoku|en|Gōzoku|WD=Q84066|lt=feudal lords|1=Gōzoku}} were granted the names of {{ill|Uji (clan)|en|Uji (clan)|WD=Q2575910|lt=Uji|1=Uji (clan)}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabane|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court of Japan|1=Royal court}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato Kingship}}). After the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reforms|1=Taika Reform}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 律令制}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient Japanese household registration system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戸籍|1=Family register|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q485228}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bemmin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=Bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2892644}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Family (Japan)|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}, and {{ill|Nobi|en|Nobi|WD=Q1146808|lt=Nobi|1=Nobi}}.

The Uji-Kabane system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as {{ill|Omi|en|Omi|WD=Q359551|lt=Omi|1=Omi}}, [[Muraji]], [[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their Rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''{{ill|Omi|en|Omi|WD=Q359551|lt=Omi|1=Omi}} (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|WD=Q909854|lt=Soga clan|1=Soga clan}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan|1=Katsuragi clan}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan|1=Mononobe clan}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan|1=Ōtomo clan (ancient)}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|en|Hata clan|WD=Q987290|lt=Hata clan|1=Hata clan}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuge clan|qq=}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The Uji-Kabane system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ranks of Titles|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into Ranks such as {{ill|Mahito|en|Mahito|WD=Q85846522|lt=Mahito|1=Mahito}} (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Uji-Kabane system == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|en|Fujiwara clan|WD=Q641628|lt=Fujiwara clan|1=Fujiwara clan}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan|1=Taira clan}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan|1=Minamoto clan}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字). == See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[:en:Family name|Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]
== References == {{reflist|20em}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Naoki, Kōjirō |title=Ancient Japanese Clans and the Emperor |publisher=[[Hanawa Shobō]] |date=1964 |isbn=978-4-8273-1012-2 |ref=Naoki 1964}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |title=The Ancient Japanese Uji-Kabane system |publisher=[[Yagi Shoten]] |date=2009 |isbn=978-4-8406-2036-9 |ref=Nakamura 2009}} * {{Cite book |language=ja |author=Nakamura, Tomokazu |editor=Sato, Makoto |title=Themes in Japanese Ancient History: Politics and Diplomacy |publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]] |date=2020 |isbn=978-4-642-08384-3 |ref=Nakamura 2020}}

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=March 2018}} {{nihongo|'''Uji-Kabane system'''|氏姓制度||onyomi: Shisei}} refers to the ancient Japanese system in which central {{ill|Nobility|en|Nobility|WD=Q134737|lt=aristocrats|1=Nobility}} and later local {{ill|Gōzoku|en|Gōzoku|WD=Q84066|lt=feudal lords|1=Gōzoku}} were granted the names of {{ill|Uji (clan)|en|Uji (clan)|WD=Q2575910|lt=Uji|1=Uji (clan)}} (uji) and {{ill|kabane|ja|姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kabane|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (kabane) by the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|WD=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court of Japan|1=Royal court}} based on their contributions to the state (the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato Kingship}}). This system allowed the privileged positions of these groups to become {{ill|Line of hereditary succession|en|Line of hereditary succession|WD=Q756689|lt=hereditary|1=Line of hereditary succession}}. The system is also known as "the Uji-Kabane system" (氏・姓の制) and is pronounced as "shisei" when read in on-yomi.
After the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|WD=Q570140|lt=Taika Reforms|1=Taika Reform}}, as the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令国家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 律令制}} state was established, the {{ill|ancient Japanese household registration system|ja|戸籍制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancient Japanese household registration system|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戸籍|1=Family register|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q485228}} extended the shisei system to cover the {{ill|bemmin|ja|部民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bemmin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 部民制|1=Bemin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2892644}} (common people), thus representing the social statuses of all classes in the nation. Those without shisei were limited to the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}, {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Family (Japan)|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581068}}, and {{ill|Nobi|en|Nobi|WD=Q1146808|lt=Nobi|1=Nobi}}.
== Uji and Kabane == In the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato Kingship}}, powerful clans served under the Great King (later known as the Emperor), forming the foundation of the ruling structure. In ancient Japan, an uji was a kinship-based group centered around a leading family line, encompassing both blood-related and non-blood-related households<ref name="Naoki1964p111">{{ill|Naoki 1964|ja|直木 1964|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naoki 1964|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 111</ref>. Members of an uji were typically blood relatives (especially in the leading family), but exceptions existed, and differences in social status were also evident within the uji. The central family line often maintained a political relationship with the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato Kingship}}, which was essential for the clan’s internal cohesion and external expansion<ref name="Naoki1964p111"/><ref name="Nakamura2009p6">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2009|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 6</ref>.
An uji, therefore, was not merely a naturally occurring kinship group (clan) but a political entity closely tied to the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26">{{ill|Nakamura 2020|ja|中村 2020|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2020|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 26</ref><ref name="Sato2021p8">{{ill|Sato 2021|ja|佐藤 2021|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato 2021|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 8</ref>. This political nature of the uji is further reflected in the fact that uji names often indicated specific occupational roles (indicating a lord-retainer relationship with the emperor), and the court retained the authority to grant or modify uji and kabane<ref name="Nakamura2009p24">{{ill|Nakamura 2009|ja|中村 2009|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakamura 2009|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, p. 24</ref>. Uji names were derived from geographic locations (e.g., {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|WD=Q909854|lt=Soga clan|1=Soga clan}}, {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan|1=Katsuragi clan}}, {{ill|Kibi clan|en|Kibi clan|WD=Q11412645|lt=Kibi clan|1=Kibi clan}}, {{ill|Kamitsuke clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsuke clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}) or occupational roles (e.g., {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan|1=Mononobe clan}}, {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan|1=Ōtomo clan (ancient)}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|en|Nakatomi clan|WD=Q1198447|lt=Nakatomi clan|1=Nakatomi clan}}). These uji were associated with kabane such as {{ill|Omi (title)|en|Omi (title)|WD=Q2574718|lt=Omi|1=Omi (title)}} (omi), {{ill|Muraji|en|Muraji|WD=Q2735611|lt=Muraji|1=Muraji}} (muraji), or {{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyatsuko|1=Q55526942|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526942}} (miyatsuko), indicating their roles within the Yamato court<ref name="Nakamura2020p26" />. == Establishment of the Uji-Kabane system == {{See|Clans in Japan|Kabane}} In primitive communities, tribes and clans formed the basic social units. The Uji-Kabane system emerged by the 5th to 6th centuries. Selected individuals within clans were granted titles such as {{ill|Omi|en|Omi|WD=Q359551|lt=Omi|1=Omi}}, [[Muraji]], [[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]], and [[Kuni no Miyatsuko]], distinguishing their Rank and responsibilities.
The titles included: * '''{{ill|Omi|en|Omi|WD=Q359551|lt=Omi|1=Omi}} (臣):''' Powerful clans like the {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|WD=Q909854|lt=Soga clan|1=Soga clan}} and {{ill|Katsuragi clan|en|Katsuragi clan|WD=Q11620908|lt=Katsuragi clan|1=Katsuragi clan}} held significant political influence alongside the Yamato sovereign. * '''[[Muraji]] (連):''' Clans like the {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|Mononobe clan|WD=Q1755836|lt=Mononobe clan|1=Mononobe clan}} and {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan|1=Ōtomo clan (ancient)}} supported the court through specialized roles. * '''[[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no Miyatsuko]] (伴造):''' Subordinates managing specific administrative functions. Examples include the {{ill|Hata clan|en|Hata clan|WD=Q987290|lt=Hata clan|1=Hata clan}} and {{ill|Yuge clan|ja|弓削氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuge clan|qq=}}. * '''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]] (国造):''' Regional leaders integrated into the Yamato Court's administrative system.
The Uji-Kabane system primarily developed as a hierarchy under the Great King, with duties divided among hereditary officeholders.
== Taika Reforms and Reorganization == The [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]] of 645 AD marked the transition from the clan-based system to a bureaucratic state. This reform aimed to unify all individuals under state authority through measures like the [[Kōshi Declaration]] of 664 AD and the establishment of the {{ill|Eight Ranks of Titles|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ranks of Titles|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} in 684 AD. These measures stratified titles into Ranks such as {{ill|Mahito|en|Mahito|WD=Q85846522|lt=Mahito|1=Mahito}} (真人), [[Asomi]] (朝臣), and [[Sukune]] (宿禰).
== Decline of the Uji-Kabane system == By the 9th century, the dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|en|Fujiwara clan|WD=Q641628|lt=Fujiwara clan|1=Fujiwara clan}} and the emergence of new aristocratic and warrior groups, such as the {{ill|Taira clan|en|Taira clan|WD=Q1079102|lt=Taira clan|1=Taira clan}} and {{ill|Minamoto clan|en|Minamoto clan|WD=Q599881|lt=Minamoto clan|1=Minamoto clan}}, marked the decline of the clan and title system. The system was eventually supplanted by practices like the establishment of family names (家名) and regional surnames (苗字). == See Also == * [[Clans in Japan]] * [[:en:Family name|Family name]] * {{ill|Eight Rank System|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Rank System|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} * [[Ritsuryō system]]
